coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable silica-coated-carbon dot-encapsulated LaCaAl3O7:Eu3+ phosphor: Bridging advanced lighting and multimodal security applications

coredata.dc:description:
Background A novel series of silica (SiO₂) coated carbon dots (CDs) grafted onto LaCaAl₃O₇:Eu³⁺ (LCAO:Eu³⁺) hybrid phosphors is synthesized using a simple solution combustion method with coconut water as the fuel. These nanocomposites (NCs) are designed to enhance the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the LCAO:Eu³⁺ matrix. CDs improved the absorption cross-section of red-emitting Eu³⁺ ions, significantly increasing luminescence intensity. The NCs exhibited color tunability and temperature sensitivity, making them suitable for applications in lighting and anticounterfeiting.
Methods The NCs are synthesized by incorporating various concentrations of CDs into the LCAO:Eu³⁺ matrix, followed by SiO₂ coating to improve PL performance. The emission spectra are tuned by adjusting the CDs concentration and excitation wavelength. Comprehensive PL characterizations are performed, focusing on quantum yield (QY), luminescence intensity, and color tunability for practical applications.
Significant Findings Incorporating CDs achieved a PL QY of 87.72 % and enhanced LCAO:3Eu³⁺ luminescence intensity 21.32-fold, further improved to 88.69-fold with SiO₂ coating. The NCs displayed tunable emission spectra (pink to deep blue) and a high CRI of 93 for white light emitting (w-LEDs). They functioned as optical thermometers with 3.02 % K⁻¹ sensitivity at 460 K. SiO₂/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu³⁺ produced unique luminescent patterns, enabling robust anticounterfeiting and multi-level encryption for authentication.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel series of silica (SiO₂) coated carbon dots (CDs) grafted onto LaCaAl₃O₇:Eu³⁺ (LCAO:Eu³⁺) hybrid phosphors is synthesized using a simple solution combustion method with coconut water as the fuel. These nanocomposites (NCs) are designed to enhance the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the LCAO:Eu³⁺ matrix. CDs improved the absorption cross-section of red-emitting Eu³⁺ ions, significantly increasing luminescence intensity. The NCs exhibited color tunability and temperature sensitivity, making them suitable for applications in lighting and anticounterfeiting.

The NCs are synthesized by incorporating various concentrations of CDs into the LCAO:Eu³⁺ matrix, followed by SiO₂ coating to improve PL performance. The emission spectra are tuned by adjusting the CDs concentration and excitation wavelength. Comprehensive PL characterizations are performed, focusing on quantum yield (QY), luminescence intensity, and color tunability for practical applications.

Incorporating CDs achieved a PL QY of 87.72 % and enhanced LCAO:3Eu³⁺ luminescence intensity 21.32-fold, further improved to 88.69-fold with SiO₂ coating. The NCs displayed tunable emission spectra (pink to deep blue) and a high CRI of 93 for white light emitting (w-LEDs). They functioned as optical thermometers with 3.02 % K⁻¹ sensitivity at 460 K. SiO₂/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu³⁺ produced unique luminescent patterns, enabling robust anticounterfeiting and multi-level encryption for authentication.

ce:section.ce:para: Multicolor luminescent materials are increasingly explored for flexible full-color displays, biological tagging, and next-generation lighting due to their tunable emissions under single-wavelength excitation and energy-efficient potential [1]. Various types, including quantum dots (QDs), polymer dots, rare-earth (RE) nanoparticles, molecular nanomaterials and organic dyes have been developed [2–4], but challenges like low quantum yields, poor water solubility, photobleaching, and complex synthesis hinder practical use [5]. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising alternatives to metal-based QDs due to their low toxicity, biocompatibility, and stable PL [6,7]. Their excitation-dependent PL makes them suitable for multicolor sensing [8], yet issues like self-quenching through FRET and π-π interactions limit their solid-state applications. Overcoming PL quenching from aggregation remains a critical challenge [9], and embedding CDs in solid matrices such as polymers or silica-based materials has proven effective. For instance, incorporating CDs into sodium borosilicate networks enhanced quantum efficiency and shifted emission properties [10], while silica-based matrices offer stability and compatibility with lighting systems [11].

w-LEDs dominate solid-state lighting due to their energy efficiency, longevity, and environmental benefits [12]. Commercial w-LEDs typically use a blue InGaN/GaN LED with a YAG:Ce yellow phosphor [13], but degradation of the blue chip and phosphor shifts can affect color output. Using UV excitation instead of blue light can mitigate these issues [14], and UV-chip-based phosphors have gained attention [15,16]. High CRI (>90) w-LEDs are especially needed in settings like museums and studios, where accurate color rendering is crucial [17]. While combining multiple phosphors broadens emission and improves CRI, it also reduces efficiency due to self-absorption and inconsistent aging of components [18]. Composite materials offer a solution for enhancing optical performance [19], and CDs often embedded in solid hosts, show promise for such applications [20]. CDs, when combined with red phosphors, enhance brightness and chromaticity in wLEDs. For instance, Liu's group achieved uniform distribution of Sr2Si5N8:Eu2+ in cellulose [21]. CaAl12O19:Mn4+ (CAO) is another cost-effective red-emitting phosphor with excellent optical properties [22]. In this study, CDs are incorporated into LaCaAl3O7:Eu3+ (CDs@LCAO:Eu3+), enabling tunable white-light emission by adjusting the excitation wavelength and CDs-to-phosphor mass ratio.

Activator ions such as Sm3+, Pr3+, and Eu2+/Eu3+ are widely used in RE doped red phosphors. Recently, melilite-structured compounds have attracted interest for their ability to host high activator concentrations [23]. Energy transfer (ET) between RE ions enhances luminescence efficiency, improves color rendering in w-LEDs, and lowers production costs, making ET-based color-tunable phosphors in melilite structures a key research area. The RE calcium aluminate family, including CaLnAl3O7 compounds (ABC3O7-type), exhibits unique structural and optical properties. LLP has been studied in CaYAl3O7:Eu2+,Dy3+ and CaGdAl3O7 [24], with Ce3+ and RE3+ (RE = Eu, Tb) luminescence also reported under VUV excitation [25,26], relevant for mercury-free HID lamps and PDPs. Further, LaCaAl3O7 hosts and GdCaAl3O7:Eu3+ with X-ray -excited luminescence have been explored [27,28]. However, LCAO has not been thoroughly investigated for full La3+ to Eu3+ substitution or its ET mechanisms. As literature indicates limited study of LCAO (Table 1 ) [29–35], this work examines the crystal structure, ET efficiency, and color-tunable luminescence of LCAO:Eu3+ with incorporated CDs.

As summarized in Table S1, extensive studies have explored the use of SiO2 as a shell material for phosphor cores [36–40]. SiO2 shells provide several advantages for luminescent NPs, including improved thermal stability, enhanced particle dispersibility, and the ability to functionalize the surface with various organic molecules. Coating phosphor surfaces with SiO2 offers a significant benefit by improving PL properties and overall stability. Additionally, silica as an outer shell layer can effectively reduce agglomeration by modifying the surface charge of spherical particles [41]. Lenka et al. [42] demonstrated that coating ZnO:Ga NPs with SiO2 reduced agglomeration and limited the growth of crystallite size during annealing, which significantly enhanced the luminescence intensity of Ga3+ ions. Moreover, due to its excellent optical transparency, biocompatibility, and ability to form covalent bonds with grafted medicinal molecules, silica is widely used as a coating material in biomedicine [43]. In recent years, the biocompatible, functional, and tunable optical properties of core-shell nanophosphors achieved through their adjustable thicknesses, shapes, and sizes have garnered considerable interest in the field of sensor research [44]. A wide range of both hard and soft materials have been strategically used to build basic shell structures. Among these, SiO2-coated structures are commonly chosen due to their optical transparency, tunable sizes, and excellent thermal and chemical stability, as well as their nanoporous nature [45]. Moreover, silica improves brightness and resolution by minimizing light scattering, making it superior to traditional materials. Its hydroxyl groups (-OH) further enhance its potential for use in biological and optoelectronic applications [46]. To improve the structural and optical properties of the composite, we fabricated a well-defined core-shell spherical structure of SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+.

Temperature significantly affects chemical and biological processes, and among various measurement methods, fluorescence-based sensing stands out for its non-invasiveness, rapid response, and resistance to electromagnetic interference [47]. This method relies on the decrease in emission intensity with increasing temperature due to thermally activated non-radiative deactivation [48], a behavior observed in materials like semiconductor nanocrystals, CDs, and RE ions [49]. Compared to single-intensity approaches, fluorescence ratiometric techniques are more reliable, offering built-in calibration by detecting two emission signals under one excitation wavelength. This enhances accuracy by minimizing errors from probe concentration and system fluctuations. The goal is to develop a composite with enhanced thermal sensitivity.

With rising counterfeiting threats, information security has become a major concern in modern society [50]. Anticounterfeiting and encryption are widely used to safeguard information [51], utilizing technologies like markers, magnetism, plasmons, holograms, and luminescence [52]. Among these, colorful PL stands out due to its high visibility, simple design, and compatibility with portable UV lamps. Recently, photoluminescent materials such as semiconductor/CDs, MOFs, dyes, and phosphors have been employed to create PL images for security. For anticounterfeiting, multicolor and multimode PL (color and intensity changes under different stimuli) are key features [53]. In encryption, PL information remains invisible under normal light and is revealed only under UV illumination. Fluorescent ink is a simple yet effective anticounterfeiting material widely used for information encoding, storage, and protection. It is typically produced by combining multiple fluorescent materials to enable multimode luminescence, enhancing security. A common approach involves integrating CDs with RE doped materials, which are valued for their low phonon energy, chemical stability, and modifiable surface structures. These features make them suitable for applications in optoelectronics, fiber lasers, and biomedical imaging. Their luminescence arises from f–f or f–d electronic transitions. CDs, with their excellent optical properties, also show strong potential for anticounterfeiting and encryption uses.

The present study introduces a highly innovative SiO2/CDs@LCAO:Eu3+ NCs, surpassing previously reported LCAO-based phosphors (Table 1) in both luminescent efficiency and multifunctional applications. While prior studies primarily explored Ce3+, Tb3+, Nd3+ and Pr3+ doped LCAO for LEDs, spectral modification, and LFP analysis, none have integrated CDs to enhance luminescence via Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). Several studies have employed the solid-state reaction method, which generally requires high-temperature processing and may result in particle agglomeration. More recent works have shifted towards solution combustion synthesis, which offers better homogeneity, lower synthesis temperatures, and improved luminescent efficiency. This study introduces a cost-effective solution combustion method to synthesize LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 0.5–5 mol %) phosphors with utilizing coconut water as a biofuel, ensuring better control over particle morphology and phase purity. Further, this work utilizes eco-friendly neem leaves powder to synthesize CDs and this CDs is integrated with synthesized phosphor, resulting in a novel color-tunable composite, achieving a 21.32-fold PL enhancement. The CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ system, coated with SiO2, further boosted PL by 88.69-fold is engineered for advanced applications including multimodal anticounterfeiting, white light-emitting diodes (wLEDs), and optical thermometry. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first comprehensive investigation exploring the impact of incorporating CDs into the LCAO:Eu3+ matrix and evaluating the role of SiO2 coating in enhancing the performance of these NCs. The challenge of achieving white light emission from multiple phosphors is addressed by developing SiO2/CDs (5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs capable of producing white light with a high color rendering index (Ra) of 93. Moreover, the study introduces SiO2/CDs (1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ multi-emissive NCs as intelligent, multimodal anticounterfeiting labels, bridging significant gaps in current research. In contrast to conventional methods, this approach employs a multi-layered encryption strategy using concealed luminescent codes that are only visible under UV light. This innovative integration of luminescence, encryption, and optical security offers promising avenues for next-generation anticounterfeiting solutions and secure packaging technologies.
Details of the experimental procedures and characterization techniques are provided in Notes 1 and 2 of the Supplementary Information (Figure S1a-e).

The phase composition and crystalline structure of the synthesized Eu3+-doped nanophosphors are confirmed via X-Ray Diffraction (XRD). The XRD profiles of LCAO and LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mol %) are shown in Fig. 1
a. The peaks are indexed with corresponding Miller indices and matched the ICDD 38–1251 [35], indicating a tetragonal phase with P
4 ¯
21m space group. Incorporation of Eu3+ ions showed no significant change in diffraction bands, confirming the nanophosphors are synthesized effectively and pure. This aligns with doping chemistry principles suggesting that chemically similar ions are more likely to be incorporated into the host lattice [54]. Eu3+ ions can substitute La3+ due to their similar charge and size, while Ca2+ cannot be replaced by Eu3+ because of differences in charge and ionic radii. To confirm the substitution, the radius percentage difference (Δr) between the host and doped cations must be calculated using the following formula [55]:
(1)
Δ
r
=
[
R m

)

100 %

In this context, Rd (CN) represents the effective ionic radius of the doped ions, Rm (CN) refers to the ionic radii of the potential substituted ions, and CN denotes the coordination number. The ionic radii of La3+ and Eu3+ are 1.216 Å and 1.12 Å, respectively, at CN = 9. With a computed Δr value of 8.57 % for Eu3+ and La3+ ions, Eu3+ can effectively replace La3+ ions in the host structure. According to the literature, effective substitution requires a minimum percentage below 30 %. Since the computed Δr value for Eu3+ and La3+ ions is well below this limit, Eu3+ ions can replace La3+ ions in the host matrix. Substituting the larger La3+ ions with smaller Eu3+ ions causes a decrease in interplanar spacing and an increase in the diffraction angle (θ). This results in a shift of peak positions towards higher angles, as seen in the magnified XRD patterns in the 2θ range of 30.5–31.7°, shown in Fig. 1 b

The XRD pattern of the synthesized CDs displayed a broad peak at approximately 2θ = 21°, attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, and a smaller peak at 2θ = 40°, corresponding to the (100) plane [56]. For CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, the broad peaks near 2θ = 21° and 40° confirmed the presence of CDs, characteristic of their amorphous structure. The remaining diffraction peaks aligned with the tetragonal structure of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, indicating the crystalline framework remained intact despite the addition of CDs (Fig. 1 c). SiO2 displayed a diffraction peak at 2θ = 23°, characteristic of its amorphous nature. For SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+, distinct peaks for both SiO2 and CDs are observed, confirming their coexistence within the composite without disrupting the primary crystalline structure. Deconvolution of the XRD pattern in the 2θ range of 18°–22° (Fig. 1 d) revealed two peaks, one attributed to CDs and the other to SiO2. This analysis confirmed the successful integration of CDs and SiO2 within the composite.

To gain further structural insights, the XRD results for Eu3+-doped LCAO samples are refined using GSAS II software [57], with LCAO parameters as initial inputs. The refined patterns for the host matrix and optimized LCAO:3Eu3+ phosphor are shown in Fig. 1 e & f, respectively. Additional patterns for other compositions are presented in Figure S2(a-f), with detailed refinement results in Table S2. The refinement parameters Rwp, Rp and χ2 are 7.65 %, 8.13 %, and 1.41, respectively, indicating that LCAO:3Eu3+ phosphors fit well within the tetragonal structure of the P
4 ¯
21m space group. The refined data is converted into CIF format using VESTA 3.5.8 software to visualize the 3D crystal structure of LCAO:3Eu3+ and the coordination environment of cations within the unit cell, as shown in Figs. 1 g & 1 h The lattice adopts tetragonal symmetry with a rectangular prism unit cell. La3+/Ca2+/Eu3+ ions (blue spheres) occupy specific lattice positions, with Eu3+ likely substituting La3+ ions. Aluminium ions (purple spheres) coordinate with oxygen atoms, forming polyhedral units. Oxygen ions (yellow spheres) stabilize the structure via ionic bonding. The Aluminum-centered tetrahedra (pink) represent AlO4 units, each Al bonded to four oxygen atoms. The La3+/Ca2+/Eu3+ centered polyhedra (blue) show coordination with surrounding oxygen atoms. These polyhedra are interconnected, contributing to the stability and integrity of the tetragonal lattice, highlighting the role of AlO4 tetrahedra and Eu3+ ions in shaping the material's structural and optical properties.

The crystallite sizes of the synthesized nanophosphors are then evaluated using the Debye-Scherrer's equation [58],
(2)
D =
k λ
β cos θ

The shape factor (k) ranges from 0.62 to 2.08, with 0.9 commonly used for spherical crystals [59]. In the Debye-Scherrer formula, λ is the X-ray wavelength (λ = 1.54 Å for CuKα), β is the peak broadening in radians, θ is the diffraction angle, and D is the crystallite size in nm. Crystallite sizes for pure and 3 mol % doped samples are 36.13 nm and 35.56 nm, respectively. The Scherrer's equation only considers crystallite size from peak broadening and overlooks defects, stacking faults, and strain [60]. Methods like the Williamson-Hall plot and Size-Strain plot address both size and strain effects [61].

The Size-Strain Plot method initially assumed that strain-induced broadening followed a Gaussian function, while size-induced broadening followed a Lorentzian function [62]. However, it is found that the peak profile combines both functions, with the peak area fitting the Gaussian and the tail resembling a Lorentzian. To address this, Halder and Wagner introduced the Halder-Wagner (H-W) model, which assumes peak broadening follows a Voigt function [63]. This model is useful as it gives more weight to peaks in the low and mid-angle regions, where diffraction peaks have minimal overlap. The full width at half maximum of the Voigt function can be represented using the H-W model,

The results, summarized in Table S3, highlight the superior crystallinity and minimal defect levels of LCAO:xEu3+ NPs, making them suitable for advanced optical applications.

The nano-dimensional characteristics of the synthesized LCAO:3Eu3+ nanophosphor, CDs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are examined using advanced techniques. The SEM images in Fig. 2 offer insights into the materials' morphology. Fig. 2 a shows the microstructure of LCAO:3Eu3+, revealing spherical particles ranging from 143.27 nm to 180.96 nm, with few voids, suggesting uniform synthesis. Fig. 2 b presents the morphology of CDs, showing uniformly distributed spherical particles with smooth surfaces. In Fig. 2 c, the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ composite is shown, with CDs well-distributed on the LCAO:3Eu3+ surface and a particle size between 189.84 and 192.01 nm. Fig. 2 e provides a closer view of the composite core, highlighting the homogeneous distribution of CDs on the host material.

Fig. 2 d shows the SEM image of SiO2-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ composite, revealing a distinct core-shell structure, with the SiO2 layer uniformly coating the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ core, indicating a controlled coating process. The magnified view in Fig. 2 f further supports this core-shell morphology, with a core diameter of 191.50 nm and a SiO2 shell thickness of 70.21 nm. The size distribution of the CDs, derived from the SEM images of the CDs and the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ composite, is shown in histograms in Figs. 2 g & h. The particles predominantly range from 3 to 8 nm, with an average size of 5.38 ± 0.23 nm for the standalone CDs and 5.51 ± 0.35 nm for the CDs in the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. These results confirm the uniformity of the particle sizes, which is crucial for maintaining consistent optical and functional properties in the materials.

Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDAX) is conducted to determine the elemental composition of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, as shown in Figure S3. The analysis of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs (Figure S3a) revealed that oxygen is the most abundant element, followed by aluminium, calcium, lanthanum, and europium. The EDAX spectra of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs (Figure S3b) confirmed the presence of carbon, indicating the integration of CDs into the structure. The deposition of the SiO2 layer in SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs (Figure S3c) is verified by the appearance of a silicon peak. Figures S3(d–h) for LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, Figure S3(i–n) for CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and S2(o–u) for SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs show elemental mappings for lanthanum, calcium, aluminium, oxygen, europium, carbon, and silicon. These maps demonstrate uniform distribution of elements across the matrices. The statistical bar graph in Figure S3v highlights variations in atomic percentages among the samples. These results confirm the effective synthesis and consistent elemental distribution in the materials.

The morphology of core and core-shell structures affects surface and physical properties. TEM analysis is conducted on LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, as shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 a reveals that LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs are mostly crystalline, with some agglomeration and a near-spherical shape. Fig. 3 b displays the CDs, which are spherical and uniformly distributed, with an average size of 5.27 ± 0.34 nm, confirming their ultrasmall dimensions and high surface area-to-volume ratios. Fig. 3 c displays the TEM image of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, confirming the successful integration of CDs into the LCAO:3Eu3+ matrix with uniform distribution, as indicated by the yellow dotted circles. This integration enhances PL properties via a FRET mechanism [67]. Fig. 3 d shows the spherical shape of the SiO2 coated core-shell particles. TEM images in Figs. 3 e-g confirm a crystalline core surrounded by an amorphous SiO2 shell, with consistent shell thicknesses of around 72.13 nm, 71.25 nm, and 74.17 nm. The uniformity of the SiO2 coating confirms the effectiveness of the process, ensuring stability and improved properties. The SiO2 layer forms a thin, continuous shell around the LCAO:3Eu3+ particles, indicating successful deposition. The core-shell structure, with LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs at the core and SiO2 as the shell, enhances stability and PL properties by preventing agglomeration.

The crystallinity of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs is shown in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 3 h). Further analysis with HR-TEM and fast Fourier transform (FFT) (Fig. 3 l) confirms the tetragonal phase of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, with a 0.291 nm interplanar distance corresponding to the (211) plane, verified by inverse FFT (IFFT) and pixel profile plot in Figure 3(l1 & l11). The HR-TEM image of the CDs (Fig. 3 i) reveals lattice fringes, while the FFT image (Fig. 3 m) shows the amorphous nature of the CDs, with an interplanar spacing of 0.352 nm corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [68]. Figures 3m1 & m11 show FFT patterns, confirming interplanar spacings of 0.291 nm and 0.352 nm for the (211) and (002) planes. The HR-TEM image of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 3 j) reveals two distinct planes, with FFT patterns in Figs. 3 n & o corresponding to the (211) and (002) planes of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs and CDs. IFFT and pixel profile analysis from GATAN digital micrograph [69] confirm the interplanar spacings, supporting the presence of CDs on the NPs (Figures 3n1 & n11, 4o1 & o11). The HR-TEM image of SiO2-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs (Fig. 3 k) shows the core-shell structure, with FFT images (Figs. 3 p-s) confirming crystallinity of the LCAO:3Eu3+ core and the amorphous SiO2 shell. Figure S1e illustrates the mechanism for core-shell formation on CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. The methoxysilane groups in the CDs generate reactive intermediates that bond with silanol and –OH groups. SiO2 encapsulated NPs are created by hydrolyzing and co-condensing the CDs with silica precursors like TEOS. The SiO2 coating forms through TEOS hydrolysis, creating an inorganic silica framework. –OH groups adsorb onto the phosphor surface, initiating precipitation. This results in a continuous silica layer, with the CDs gradually embedding into the silica shell [70].

Figure S4a shows the FT-IR spectrum of LCAO:3Eu3+, with peaks at 750.85 cm-1 and 595.45 cm-1 confirming metal-oxygen (La-O, Ca-O, and Al-O) stretching vibrations. Figure S4b displays the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, where the peak at 3412.27 cm-1 corresponds to O–H stretching, 2885.72 cm-1 to CH vibrations, 1621.42 cm-1 to C = O in COOH, 1459.02 cm-1 to C = C bending, 1156.05 cm-1 to CN bending, and 839.98 cm-1 to asymmetric C–O vibrations [71]. FT-IR measurements show that the CDs are functionalized with nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups. Upon incorporating CDs, new peaks and shifts are observed (Figure S4c). The peak at 3410.51 cm-1 corresponds to O–H stretching, while peaks at 1623.01 cm-1 and 1460.09 cm-1 represent C = O and C = C bending vibrations, indicating functional groups on the LCAO:3Eu3+ surface. Peaks at 1159.16 cm-1 and 838.63 cm-1 correspond to CN and CO vibrations. Figure S4d shows the FT-IR spectrum of SiO2, with peaks at 1084.34 cm-1, 810.12 cm-1, and 479.13 cm-1 for Si-O-Si symmetric, anti-symmetric stretching and Si-O bending [72]. Figure S4e displays the FT-IR spectrum of SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. The spectrum reveals the characteristic peaks of LCAO:3Eu3+, CDs, along with Si-O-Si absorption at 1085.28 cm-1, anti-symmetric stretching at 811.03 cm-1, and Si-O bending at 479.91 cm-1, confirming the formation of a silica layer around the core. These results indicate that the CDs can bond with the silanol and hydroxyl groups in the silica-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs.

X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis further explored the chemical compositions of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. The survey scan XPS spectra are shown in Figure S5a. High-resolution XPS spectra of La 3d (Figure S5b) exhibit a characteristic doublet splitting, with peaks at ∼834.02 eV and 837.38 eV (La 3d5/2) and ∼851.28 eV and 854.90 eV (La 3d3/2), indicating the presence of La3+ across all samples. Figure S5c displays the Ca 2p spectra, with peaks at 347.21 eV and 350.64 eV corresponding to the Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 core levels, respectively, confirming the 2+ oxidation state of Ca. The Al 2p spectra in Figure S5d show a peak at ∼74 eV, attributed to Al–O bonding and confirming the formation of aluminum oxides.

The O 1 s spectra (Figure S5e) reveal a peak at ∼532.78 eV, attributed to O–H bonds, indicating hydroxyl groups on the LCAO:3Eu3+ surface. These hydroxyl functionalities enhance hydrophilicity and reactivity. Another peak at ∼531.82 eV corresponds to O–Al bonds, indicative of aluminum oxides or hydroxides. An increase in oxygen-related species in CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ suggests higher surface reactivity, likely due to CD aggregation, which could enhance the composites' performance. Peaks at 1136.03 eV (Eu 3d5/2) and 1163.59 eV (Eu 3d3/2) in the Eu 3d spectra (Figure S5f) confirm the presence of Eu3+ ions. The C 1 s spectra (Figure S5g) reveal two peaks for LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, likely from residual carbon from stabilizers used during synthesis. CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs exhibit three distinct peaks: 284.75 eV for C–C bonds (sp2 carbon in aromatic structures), 286.11 eV for C–O bonds (indicating hydroxyl groups), and 289.92 eV for C = O bonds (carboxyl groups) [73]. These results confirm the oxygen-containing functionalities of CDs. The N 1s spectra (Figure S5h) show a peak at 399.83 eV, attributed to amino nitrogen (N–H), observed exclusively in CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. This indicates the incorporation of amine groups, which enhance interactions within the composites. The Si 2p spectra (Figure S5i) for SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs reveal peaks at 101.80 eV and 102.47 eV, corresponding to Si–C and Si–O bonds, confirming the successful formation of SiO2 around CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. In CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, slight shifts in binding energies of La 3d, Ca 2p, Al 2p, O 1s, Eu 3d, N 1s, and C 1s orbitals suggest changes in the chemical environment, likely due to the influence of CDs and SiO2. These shifts reflect alterations in the local electronic structure, which may affect bonding and charge distribution, ultimately influencing the composite's overall properties [74].

The 614 nm peak displays a vivid red emission, characteristic of hypersensitive ED transitions, which dominate the emission spectrum, suggesting Eu3+ ions occupy sites with reduced symmetry in the host lattice. The observed splitting in the emission peaks confirms the high crystallinity of the combustion-synthesized materials. The wavenumber shifts in Table S4 indicate overlap between the 2p orbital of oxygen and the 4f orbital of Eu3+. A positive δ value confirms covalent bonding between Eu3+ and O2- ions [79]. This analysis shows that Eu3+ ions in the LCAO host matrix are covalently bonded to oxygen ligands.

Fig. 4 c illustrates the emission spectra for varying concentrations of Eu3+. The inset shows the concentration quenching behavior of LCAO:xEu3+ (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mol %) doped phosphor. As the Eu3+ concentration increases, the emission intensity rises up to 3 mol %. However, beyond this concentration, the emission intensity decreases due to concentration quenching, a well-known phenomenon extensively studied in the mid-twentieth century. The energy transfer models proposed by Förster, Dexter, and Schulman are still widely used by researchers. These theories explain concentration quenching as non-radiative energy transfer between luminescent centers, driven by electric multipolar or exchange interactions. To assess this, the critical distance (R
c ) for energy transfer between Eu3+ ions and the LCAO host must be determined. This distance is calculated using Blasse's theory [80]
(11)

)

The formula uses V for the unit cell volume, X c for the Eu3+ ion concentration, and N for available activator sites. After structure refinement, the unit cell volume is 323.89 Å3, and N is 5. With Xc set to 0.03, the calculated R
c is 16.03 Å, exceeding 5 Å. This indicates that concentration quenching results from non-radiative transitions between the host lattice and rare earth ions, driven by electric multipolar interactions [81]. Reisfeld's approximation and Dexter proposed that to confirm the energy transfer mechanism between activator ions, it is necessary to analyze the emission intensity (I) in relation to the activator ion concentration (x), as described by the following equation [82].

For a given matrix with identical excitation conditions, constants K, β, and c are used. Van Uitert's theory associates θ values of 3, 6, 8, and 10 with specific interactions, including exchange, dipole-dipole (d-d), dipole-quadrupole (d-q), and quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q) interactions. Only three points from the optimized concentration are used for the linear fit. Fig. 4 d's plot of log(x) vs log(I/x) yields a slope of −2.93343, resulting in a θ value of approximately 6. The primary cause of concentration quenching in LCAO:Eu3+ phosphors is attributed to dipole-dipole interactions. These interactions suggest efficient energy transfer within the host matrix. The extended critical distance supports multipolar interactions, allowing effective energy migration with minimal non-radiative losses. At optimal dopant concentrations, this leads to enhanced luminescence. Quenching losses are minimized at higher concentrations, maximizing luminescence performance.

Fig. 4 e presents the normalized excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. The broad excitation band at 339 nm is due to the n-π⁎ transitions of C = O bonds in the CDs [83]. Upon excitation at 339 nm, the CDs emit blue fluorescence with a peak at 420 nm. This PL behavior results from surface energy traps and size fluctuations in the CDs. These factors facilitate fluorescence via emissions from surface defects and π-domain band gap transitions [84]. Under 393 nm excitation, the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs displayed both the characteristic emissions of CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, as shown in Fig. 4 f. By adjusting the excitation wavelength from 303 nm to 403 nm, the emission of the NCs could be controlled. Fig. 4 g shows that the emission peaks of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs remained stable, while the CDs’ emission peaks shifted red as the excitation wavelength increased up to 393 nm. The color footprints moved from (0.362, 0.315) to (0.252, 0.317) across the white light emission region, as depicted by the CIE chromaticity coordinates in Fig. 4 h The results demonstrate that altering the excitation wavelength can achieve white light emission. The CDs emitted strong blue light under UV excitation, with a PL Quantum Yield (QY) of 55 %, while the red-emitting LCAO:3Eu3+ phosphors had a PL QY of 82 %. After incorporating 1 wt % CDs into LCAO:3Eu3+, the PL QY of the composite reached 87.72 %. Fig. 4 i presents the PL emission properties of the composite, adjusted by varying the CDs concentration (1, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 25 wt %) in the LCAO:3Eu3+ red-emitting material under 393 nm excitation. The spectra show emissions from both NPs and CDs, with the blue emission component increasing as the concentration of CDs rises. By adjusting the red-to-blue emission ratio, the luminous colors of the NCs can shift from red to blue. Increasing the CD concentration broadens the emission band due to self-absorption, where each Stokes shift reduces the energy level for subsequent absorption, causing a red shift [85]. The NCs show higher integrated PL intensity than LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, regardless of excitation wavelength. This enhancement is attributed to Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between Eu3+ ions and CDs, enabling efficient energy transfer and improved luminescence. Numerous studies have investigated CD-based FRET systems, showcasing their promising potential in various applications due to enhanced emission intensity from FRET between CDs and Eu3+ [86]. For effective FRET, three conditions must be satisfied: (1) The donor (CDs) and acceptor (Eu3+) must be within 1–10 nm, as FRET efficiency declines with distance. (2) A significant overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's excitation spectrum is essential for energy transfer. (3) The donor and acceptor dipole orientations should be nearly parallel for optimal energy transfer. SEM, TEM, and HR-TEM images confirm the structural proximity required for effective FRET.

The excitation spectrum of Eu3+ ions (200–550 nm) overlaps with the CDs emission spectrum (350–600 nm), as shown in Fig. 4 j, enabling efficient FRET. This alignment confirms energy transfer between the donor (CDs) and acceptor (Eu3+ ions), enhancing the emission intensity of the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. The effective donor-acceptor interaction significantly improves the overall luminescence of the composite. Fig. 4 k outlines energy transfer pathways between CDs and Eu3+ ions. Under 393 nm excitation, CDs transfer energy to Eu3+ via non-radiative FRET, exciting Eu3+ electrons from the 7F0 ground state to higher levels. CDs undergo HOMO to LUMO transitions, using absorbed photon energy. This process enhances emissions as Eu3+ electrons relax to the 5D0→7F1-4 levels.

The PL spectra of LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are shown in Fig. 5
a. Incorporating CDs into the LCAO:3Eu3+ matrix enhanced PL intensity by 21.32-fold, while SiO2 coating further increased it by 4.16-fold (Fig. 5 b). The SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs exhibit 88.69 times higher PL intensity than LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs. This enhancement is attributed to surface passivation, reducing defects and non-radiative recombination centers, thus improving luminescence efficiency. Surface imperfections act as trap states, enabling non-radiative decay and quenching the PL. Silica coating passivates these defects by forming a protective layer around the CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, reducing non-radiative decay channels and enhancing radiative recombination, thus improving PL. The silica layer also minimizes non-radiative energy transfer between CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, improving overall PL efficiency. Additionally, it prevents aggregation and improves the dispersion of NCs, further enhancing their photoluminescent properties. Aggregation leads to PL quenching due to increased energy transfer between particles, resulting in non-radiative decay. The silica coating prevents aggregation by providing a protective barrier around the particles, maintaining dispersion and reducing quenching. This enhances PL intensity. Additionally, the silica layer modifies the local dielectric environment, boosting the local electromagnetic field. The refractive index contrast between the silica coating and surrounding medium increases radiative transition rates, further enhancing PL emission. The silica coating protects CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs from photo-oxidation, preventing defects that could degrade their PL over time. By reducing exposure to oxygen and moisture, it stabilizes the luminescence. The coating also improves energy transfer efficiency between the CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+, enhancing interaction between the donor and acceptor. This increased energy transfer boosts PL output. Consequently, these factors collectively result in higher PL intensity and improved stability of the luminescent properties in the NCs [36,37].

To verify energy transfer from CDs to LCAO:3Eu3+ in NCs, we analyzed the luminescence decay curves of CDs before and after their integration into LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs. Figure S6a displays the decay curves for CDs, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs recorded at 420 nm emission. The decay curve of the CDs follows a single exponential function fit [87]:

The emission decay of CDs doped samples exhibits a double exponential pattern, indicating two relaxation pathways. Similar to silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs), this decay is influenced by surface and core states. The short-lived component (τ
1 ) relates to surface interactions, while the long-lived component (τ
2 ) corresponds to core state transitions. The energy transfer between the donor (CDs) and acceptor (LCAO:3Eu3+) alters the decay profile, highlighting the interaction between electronic states within the CDs and their effect on luminescence. The average decay times for CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are 6.41 ns and 4.10 ns, respectively, which are lower than that of pure CDs. This reduction confirms energy transfer in both coated and uncoated samples.

We also recorded the luminescent decay curves of LCAO:3Eu3+, CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs at λemi = 614 nm to determine the lifetime values (Figure S6b). The decay curves are accurately fitted using a double-exponential Eq. (15). The analysis yielded decay times of 18.38 ms for LCAO:3Eu3+, 32.15 ms for CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and 25.09 ms for SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. Compared to LCAO:3Eu3+, the decay time of LCAO decreased in the silica matrix. However, the addition of CDs resulted in a slight increase in the decay time of LCAO:3Eu3+. This variation is attributed to FRET between CDs and NPs, confirming energy transfer between CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs. The calculated average lifetimes for uncoated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are 32.15 ms and 25.09 ms, respectively. The observed decrease in lifetime values with the SiO2 coating suggests that the SiO2 shell enhances the refractive index of the surrounding medium. This increase in refractive index may be responsible for the reduced radiative lifetime of Eu3+ ion electronic transitions [89]. Detailed decay times are presented in Table S5, providing further evidence of the energy transfer mechanism.

Further, the FRET efficiency can be defined by the lifetimes of the donor in the presence (τ

The calculated FRET efficiency is provided in the Table S6. As the concentration of CDs increases from 1 to 25 wt %, the average lifetime of the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs (λemi = 420 nm) steadily rises from 1.24 ns to 6.41 ns. Simultaneously, the FRET efficiency decreases significantly from 83.57 % at 1 wt % to 15.09 % at 25 wt %, indicating a reduction in energy transfer efficiency. This trend suggests that higher CD concentrations lead to increased donor lifetime and diminished FRET efficiency, likely due to greater donor-acceptor distances. This confirms the increase in CDs emission compared to Eu3+ emission as the concentration of CDs rises (Fig. 4i).

Figure S7 compares the PL intensity of SiO2 coated and uncoated samples under water immersion, ultrasonication, and UV exposure. The SiO2 coated samples show better stability and higher fluorescence efficiency over time. Uncoated samples, however, experience a more rapid decline in PL intensity. This difference is due to the protective effect of the SiO2 coating, which prevents environmental and mechanical degradation.

Figure S7a & b show the impact of water treatment on the PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. After 48 h of water exposure, uncoated samples retained only 60.12 % and 82.94 % of their original PL intensity for CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+ emission, respectively. In contrast, SiO2 coated samples maintained 90.82 % and 94.37 % of their initial PL intensity as shown in Figure S7c. This demonstrates that the SiO2 coating significantly enhances the moisture-resistance stability of the NCs. The enhanced stability of SiO2-coated NCs is due to the protective barrier formed by the SiO2 shell, which prevents water from interacting with the core material. This reduces surface oxidation and chemical reactions that could lead to luminescence quenching [91]. In conclusion, the SiO2 coating effectively improves moisture resistance, enhancing the durability and long-term performance of luminescent NCs in aqueous environments.

SiO2-coated and uncoated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are subjected to 8 h of ultrasonication (Figure S7d & e). The cavitation forces during ultrasonication cause surface erosion, structural deformation, and particle aggregation in uncoated samples, reducing PL intensity. After 8 h, the retained PL intensity is 68.80 % for CDs and 86 % for LCAO:3Eu3+, compared to their initial values. SiO2 coated samples, on the other hand, exhibit improved mechanical stability during ultrasonication. The silica layer protects the core material from mechanical damage, maintaining structural integrity and preventing particle aggregation. As a result, the coated samples experience a slower decrease in PL intensity, retaining 91.44 % for CDs and 95.27 % for LCAO:3Eu3+, as shown in Figure S7f.

The PL stability of uncoated and SiO2-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs is tested under UV exposure (36 mW/cm2) for up to 80 min. The spectra in Figure S7g & h show no significant shifts in peak positions for the coated samples. After 80 min, uncoated samples retained 77.80 % and 89.72 % of their original intensity for CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+, respectively. In contrast, SiO2-coated samples maintained 91.89 % and 94.81 % of their initial intensity for CDs and LCAO:3Eu3+, respectively (Figure S7i). The SiO2 shell slows down UV-induced degradation by acting as a physical barrier, reducing UV photon interaction with the core material. This minimizes surface defects and photodegradation, enhancing the stability of SiO2 coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. As a result, the coated NCs exhibit greater resistance to UV damage, making them suitable for prolonged UV exposure. The SiO2 coating improves UV stability, extending their potential for optical and security applications.

Color coordinates are crucial for evaluating phosphor performance. The chromaticity coordinates of LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mol %) phosphors, shown in Fig. 5 c, fall near the orange-red zone. These coordinates represent the light source's color response in the CIE color space. Color purity (CP), a key optical property, is measured to assess the emitted light's purity, ranging from 1 % to 100 %. The CP can be calculated using the following equation [92]:
(18)
CP =
(

)

)

−

)
2
×

%

In this context, (x, y) represents the CIE coordinates derived from the CIE diagram, and (xd, yd) corresponds to the dominant wavelength coordinates. To find the dominant wavelength, a horizontal line is drawn from the white light point to the CIE reference point on the plot. The equal illuminant point, denoted as (xi, yi), has coordinates (0.333, 0.333). The color purity of the optimized NPs is determined to be 98.5 %. Correlated Color Temperature (CCT) is a crucial photometric property of phosphor materials, representing the warmth or coolness of the emitted light. A CCT value greater than 4000 K indicates cooler light, while a value <3200 K indicates warmer light. The CCT value can be determined using McCamy's formula, as follows [93]:
(19)
CCT = −
437
(

3
)
+ 3601
(

)

The CCT values are determined using the color coordinates (x = 0.681, y = 0.310) for LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs and the chromaticity epicenter (xe = 0.332, ye = 0.185). The calculated CCT values, presented in Table 2 , show that the developed phosphor material emits light in the cooler zone, making it ideal for a variety of domestic applications. White-light emission is typically achieved by combining red-emitting phosphors with commercial blue and green-emitting phosphors. In this study, an efficient white-emitting composite is developed by combining blue-emitting CDs with red-emitting LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs. The phosphors are color-tunable by adjusting the concentration of CDs. The CIE and CCT diagrams for the synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 5 (c, f-h). The CIE coordinates of (0.162, 0.195) position the synthesized CDs in the blue region, while the coordinates (0.681, 0.310) position pure LCAO:3Eu3+ in the red region. Samples with varying CDs concentrations (1, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 25 wt %) are analyzed, showing a color range from pink (0.589, 0.290) to dark blue (0.165, 0.240), as shown in Table 2. Fig. 5 d & e displays the photographs of phosphors under 365 nm UV light. By adjusting the CDs content, the color composition can be tuned for white emission standards. These findings suggest that CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are promising for w-LED fabrication. Coating the composites with SiO2 shifted the chromaticity coordinates of SiO2/CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs towards the white region (x = 0.324, y = 0.309), as shown in the luminescent images in Fig. 5 e. This shift is due to the increased intensity from the SiO2 coating. Similar results are observed for all CDs(x = 1, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs, where the intensity rose and CIE coordinates shifted. The corresponding CCT values are provided in Table 2.

The dual emission spectrum of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs makes them suitable for self-referencing luminescent thermometers, offering more accurate readings than single-parameter sensors through self-calibration. To evaluate their potential, we measured the temperature-dependent emission spectra of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ from 320 to 460 K under 393 nm excitation. The resulting spectra are shown in Fig. 6
a & b. The temperature-dependent luminescence of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ showed a decrease in intensity with rising temperature. The CDs emission at 420 nm and LCAO:3Eu3+ at 614 nm both gradually weakened, likely due to non-radiative recombination activation. Additionally, the emission peak of the CDs at 450 nm red-shifted, mainly due to increased phonon scattering at higher temperatures [94]. The emission peak at 614 nm decreases slightly with temperature, while the 420 nm peak shows a more noticeable decline. Fig. 6 c & d show the relative intensity changes for both peaks in CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ samples. At 420 K, the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ sample loses 26 % and 12 % intensity for the 420 nm and 614 nm peaks, respectively. The SiO2-coated CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ loses only 20 % and 6.3 % intensity for the respective CDs and Eu3+ peaks. This difference is due to the protective SiO2 coating, which acts as a thermal barrier, reducing temperature-induced quenching effects. The coating reduces non-radiative relaxation by shielding the luminescent centers from thermal vibrations and preventing the degradation of surface states, thus preserving optical properties at higher temperatures. This emphasizes the significance of surface passivation in enhancing the thermal stability of luminescent materials. The corresponding contour plots of the emission spectra at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 6 e & f. Table 3
summarizing the relative emission intensity at 420 K for various reported systems [88,93,95,97]. Our SiO₂/CDs@LCAO:3Eu³⁺ composite demonstrates a relative intensity of 93.7 %, which is higher than or comparable to similar systems, indicating improved thermal stability and performance. This further supports the potential of our material for high-temperature luminescent applications.

Figure S8a & b show the temperature-dependent changes in the full width at half maximum (FWHM) for the characteristic peaks in the PL spectra of CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+. The FWHM values for peaks at 575 nm, 590 nm, 614 nm, 655 nm, and 710 nm remain stable, while the 420 nm peak shows an increase in FWHM with rising temperature. This broadening is more significant in the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ sample, attributed to stronger electron-phonon coupling and more non-radiative transitions at elevated temperatures [98]. The variation in the 420 nm emission peak is minimized after applying SiO2 coating to the CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ sample. The SiO2 layer acts as a thermal buffer, stabilizing the structure and reducing the effects of thermal vibrations on the CDs. It also passivates surface defects sensitive to temperature, thus limiting their contribution to band broadening. This results in more thermally stable emission with minimal FWHM variation. Overall, the SiO2 coating enhances the thermal robustness and spectral stability of the CDs. The intensity ratio of the emissions at 420 nm (F420) and 614 nm (F614) is used as a thermometric parameter in ratiometric luminescence temperature sensing for CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+. The temperature dependence of the F420/F614 ratio is shown in Figure S8c & d, demonstrating good linearity from 320 to 460 K. This relationship can be expressed as:
(20)
F 420
F 614
= 3.909 − 0.005 T
(21)
F 420
F 614
= 4.186 − 0.0061 T

In this case, T denotes the temperature range from 320 K to 460 K. The relative sensitivity for ratiometric luminescent temperature sensing is determined using the following formula [99]:
(22)
S R
=
∂
(
I 420
I 614
)
∂ T
I 420
I 614

The maximum sensitivity of 2.42 % K-1 and 3.02 % K-1 for CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs is observed at 460 K. This demonstrates that SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are suitable for temperature sensing in the 320 to 460 K range. The temperature dependence of the relative sensitivity is shown in Figures S8e & f. Figure S8g summarizing the SR values of our sensor alongside previously reported systems [100–109]. As shown, the SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ composite exhibits a high relative sensitivity of 3.02 % K-1 in the 320–460 K range, which is superior to other reported materials. This highlights the excellent thermal sensitivity and practical applicability of our composite for optical thermometry.

The CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ and SiO2/CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs are combined with PDMS, cast onto a n-UV LED chip, and cured in a round mold [110]. Figs. 7
a & b show the electroluminescence spectra, with emission bands at 420 nm (CDs) and 614 nm. The CIE diagram in Fig. 7 c shows the coordinates (0.319, 0.289) for CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ and (0.325, 0.309) for SiO2/CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+, both near the equal energy point (0.333, 0.333). CCT is an important parameter for evaluating the luminescent performance of w-LEDs. Typically, a lamp emitting warm light has a CCT below 3200 K, while a lamp emitting cold light has a CCT above 4000 K. Both fabricated w-LEDs fall into the cool LED category, with CCT values exceeding 4000 K. The w-LEDs, before and after coating, are shown in Fig. 7 d. The Color Rendering Index (CRI) is key to evaluating w-LED performance. Fig. 7 e compares the CRI of the prepared wLEDs with commercial w-LEDs, showing superior color rendering in the former. The SiO2/CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs exhibit an Ra of 93, surpassing other w-LEDs listed in Table 4 [111–116]. Table 4 summarizes the CCT and Ra of SiO₂/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ composite alongside other reported white-light-emitting phosphor systems. Present composite exhibits a CCT of 5918 K and a high Ra of 93, outperforming or matching other materials in terms of color quality. These results further demonstrate the strong potential of our composite for high-performance w-LED applications.

In recent years, the unauthorized replication of important documents and goods, including certificates, currency, well-known brands, medications, and food items, has become a major global challenge. This issue has caused serious repercussions, impacting social development, disrupting the global economy, and posing risks to human health [117]. Efforts are currently underway to combat counterfeiting through stringent regulations in the capital market, while ongoing research seeks to address these challenges with advanced security measures. One such measure is multimode anticounterfeiting security ink, which provides effective protection against various fraudulent practices. The unique multimode emission properties of synthesized SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs make them ideal for anticounterfeiting and encryption applications. The developed luminescent security inks offer several advantages, including cost-effectiveness, efficient phosphor dispersion, high luminescent intensity, and strong adhesion to various surfaces. We tested the versatility of the security inks by applying them to various surfaces using a brush technique. Fig. 8
a illustrates the anticounterfeiting application of inks with different SiO2/CDs(x)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs concentrations (x = 1 wt %, 3 wt %, 15 wt %, and 25 wt %). Each concentration produced a distinct luminescent color under UV light: pink (1 wt %), purple (3 wt %), cyan blue (15 wt %), and deep blue (25 wt %). The inks successfully created detailed anticounterfeiting labels with floral, rose, peacock, and ornamental designs. These results demonstrate the potential of these inks for security labeling applications.

SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, and 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security inks enable the creation of anticounterfeiting labels and hidden patterns visible only under UV light, making them hard to replicate. Fig. 8 b showcases handwritten labels made with different ink concentrations, resulting in multicolor designs. The varying concentrations produce distinct fluorescence intensities, which can serve as unique encryption keys. These controlled fluorescence profiles act as authentication markers, enhancing security. The inks demonstrate strong potential for secure labeling applications. Fig. 8 c demonstrates anticounterfeiting labels drawn on various surfaces, such as ceramic, plastic, craft paper, and leaf, using a brush technique. Under visible light, the labels remain invisible, but under 365 nm UV light, they emit bright luminescence, revealing intricate patterns and encrypted information. These labels work effectively across different substrates and lighting conditions, providing a reliable method for verifying authenticity. The fluorescence response enhances the security of labeled items against counterfeiting, offering an easily detectable means of identification. The synthesized NCs demonstrate excellent solubility and dispersion in PVA, making them ideal for security ink applications, particularly in anticounterfeiting. SiO2 coated NCs are promising for developing cost-effective, simple security inks that can effectively conceal information and prevent counterfeiting. The brush application technique, compared to screen printing or pen sketching, offers advantages in creating intricate designs. It is simpler, more efficient, and cost-effective, making it ideal for detailed patterns and labels. This versatility enhances the ink's applicability across various sectors [118].

Fig. 9 shows the stability tests conducted on anticounterfeiting labels made with SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, and 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security inks. The tests included solvent resistance, abrasion resistance, storage stability, and adhesion. In the solvent resistance test for SiO2/CDs(25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security ink (Fig. 9 a), labels are exposed to solvents ranging from pH 1 to 13. The text "BE A GIVER," written with the 25 wt % security ink, remained visible under UV light in all conditions. However, under highly acidic (pH = 1) and basic (pH = 13) conditions, the luminescence intensity decreased significantly, and parts of the label are erased. The ink's resilience in various solvents demonstrates its strong chemical stability in acidic, neutral, and basic environments, which is crucial for anticounterfeiting applications in diverse storage conditions, such as pharmaceutical, food, and luxury packaging. The abrasion test (Fig. 9 b) evaluates the mechanical durability of anticounterfeiting labels made with SiO2/CDs(3 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security ink on a marble surface under repeated abrasion. Despite the abrasion cycles, the luminescence intensity and design remained intact, confirming the ink's strong adhesion and resistance to mechanical stress. The labels maintain their integrity even when subjected to handling, cleaning, or general wear, making them suitable for long-lasting anticounterfeiting measures. The luminescent stability of SiO2/CDs(15 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security ink is tested on craft paper over 90 days (Fig. 9 c), showing no visible degradation, demonstrating its reliability for long-term uses such as document authentication, currency printing, and archival materials. This stability ensures the ink will not fade or lose functionality over time. Additionally, the adhesion strength of SiO2/CDs(1 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security ink is evaluated on a steel plate by applying and removing adhesive tape multiple times (Fig. 9 d). The design remained intact, highlighting the ink's resistance to tampering, a crucial feature for high-value items like electronics and luxury goods.

Fig. 10 presents stability tests on multimodal anticounterfeiting labels made with SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security inks, evaluating their robustness under various environmental and physical stresses. In the UV treatment (Fig. 10 a), the labels showed minimal changes in color, intensity, and pattern after 120 min of exposure, demonstrating excellent photostability. Temperature treatment (Fig. 10 b) at 125 °C also showed negligible changes in luminescence, highlighting the labels' stability at high temperatures. These results indicate the durability of the labels under challenging conditions, making them suitable for secure applications. The multimodal anticounterfeiting labels remained unaffected by water immersion for up to 30 min, preserving their luminescence (Fig. 10 c). Additionally, exposure to ultrasonic vibrations showed that the labels retained their luminescent properties, demonstrating high mechanical stability (Fig. 10 d).

The stability of the multimodal anticounterfeiting labels using SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security inks is due to the synergistic interaction of components, including the PVA matrix. PVA serves as a stabilizer and binder, creating a flexible polymer network that encapsulates the SiO2 matrix, CDs, and LCAO:3Eu3+ luminescent elements. This network improves surface adhesion and prevents leaching during environmental or mechanical stress. Additionally, the SiO2 matrix provides chemical inertness and thermal stability, shielding the luminescent materials from UV radiation and high temperatures [119]. CDs offer strong photostability and aggregation resistance, while Eu3+ ions ensure consistent luminescence. PVA's hydrophilic nature and excellent film-forming ability prevent water penetration, maintaining luminescence even after prolonged immersion [120]. The composite's strong interfacial bonding, aided by PVA, ensures mechanical stability during ultrasonication. These properties make the anticounterfeiting labels durable and stable for real-world use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent-based multi-level encryption for product authentication
ce:section.ce:para:
Counterfeit products often endanger consumer safety by failing to meet established safety standards, posing significant health risks. Moreover, counterfeiting supports illegal activities, disrupts global trade, and infringes on intellectual property rights [121]. Addressing counterfeiting is essential for ensuring fairness in business, safeguarding consumer interests, and maintaining a healthy economic environment. In comparison to traditional authentication methods, which often require costly and precise instruments, SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ security inks in paper disc-based sensors provide advantages such as high security with multi-level encryption, versatility, low cost, and rapid detection capabilities. This study introduces a novel product authentication method based on the unique luminescent properties of SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. The security inks are used to coat paper disks, creating four visually identical disks with distinct luminescence characteristics visible only under UV light, as shown in Fig. 11
a. These disks form the core of a multi-level security system, and by arranging them in specific combinations, we have developed a four-step code verification process. In each step, a disk is illuminated with UV light, and the resulting luminescence pattern is compared to a predetermined sequence. Fig. 11 b outlines the product authentication procedure, which begins at the manufacturing stage. The manufacturer integrates a specific combination of luminescent disks into the product packaging or product itself, and the consumer uses a UV light to verify authenticity by checking the luminescence pattern of each disk in the designated order during unboxing. The observed luminescence pattern is then compared with a unique or reference code provided by the manufacturer, enhancing the security of the authentication system. This multi-step verification process makes it difficult for counterfeiters to reproduce the correct sequence, even if they know the luminescent colors, significantly increasing the challenge of counterfeiting. The encryption system offers several advantages: high security due to unique luminescence patterns and the complexity of the process, versatility for adaptation across various products and industries (e.g., packaging, labels, or products), and user-friendliness with clear instructions for code verification. Thus, SiO2/CDs(1, 3, 15, 25 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs play a crucial role in combating counterfeiting and ensuring product authenticity across industries.

A solution combustion method is used to synthesize LCAO:xEu3+ (x = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 mol %) with coconut water as an eco-friendly fuel. Blue-emitting CDs are synthesized from dried neem leaves using microwave assistance. These CDs are grafted onto the optimized LCAO:3Eu3+ matrix, and SiO2 is coated on CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs through TEOS hydrolysis. XRD analysis confirmed tetragonal LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs, with a peak at 23° indicating SiO2 coating. The CDs showed distinct (002) and (100) planes in the XRD pattern. FTIR spectra confirmed the successful incorporation of CDs into LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs and the silica coating. SEM and TEM images revealed spherical particles with CDs around 5.27 nm in diameter and confirmed the core-shell structure of SiO2/CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs. HR-TEM further showed distinct planes in the composite, with interplanar spacings matching those of the individual components. XPS analysis identified La 3d, Ca 2p, Al 2p, O1 s, Eu 3d, C 1s, N 1s, and Si 2p orbitals in the NCs. Upon excitation at 393 nm, the LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs emitted red light at 614 nm due to the 5
D
0
→
7
F
2 transition. The CDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL with a peak emission at 420 nm under 339 nm excitation. The luminescence enhancement is attributed to energy transfer via the FRET mechanism, leading to a 21.32-fold increase in PL intensity. By varying the CDs concentration (1, 3, 5, 10, 15, and 25 wt %), the color of the NCs shifts from pink to deep blue. The SiO2 coating on CDs@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs enhances PL intensity by 4.16-fold, resulting in a total 88.69-fold increase compared to LCAO:3Eu3+ NPs. These NCs are used for temperature sensing with dual emission features, offering greater precision. SiO2/CDs(5 wt %)@LCAO:3Eu3+ NCs emit white light, making them suitable for w-LEDs. The remaining NCs (1, 3, 15, and 25 wt %) are employed in advanced multimodal applications. The inks are used to create luminescent markers that form the basis of a robust encryption system. The multi-level authentication process, which utilizes unique luminescence patterns and their specific arrangement, significantly enhances security by making it much more difficult for counterfeiters to replicate the original product. Additionally, stability tests demonstrated the inks' exceptional durability under various environmental stresses, including UV exposure, temperature fluctuations, water immersion, and ultrasonic vibrations. These findings highlight the potential of this method for developing secure anti-counterfeiting solutions, ensuring the protection of consumer interests and the integrity of legitimate businesses across the globe.
